primary_diagnosis.name: Metastatic prostate carcinoma with malignant spinal cord compression

comorbidities.name: Secondary malignant neoplasm of bone (spinal metastases)

pathophysiology_summary: Adenocarcinoma of the prostate, long occult behind lower urinary tract symptoms the patient attributed to ageing, has metastasised to the axial skeleton (a strongly osteotropic pattern for prostate cancer). Vertebral metastases in the lower thoracic/lumbar spine have produced the 3-month deep bone pain and, tonight, a pathological/epidural mass effect causing malignant spinal cord compression. Cord compression produces the classic triad: severe localised (percussion-tender) back pain, progressive bilateral leg weakness, and a sensory level — here an upper-motor-neuron pattern (brisk reflexes, bilateral extensor plantar responses) localising a central cord lesion above the conus, distinct from a peripheral radiculopathy. The compressed cord is ischaemic and dying; neurological deficit becomes irreversible over hours, so emergency imaging, high-dose corticosteroid and definitive decompression (surgery or radiotherapy) are time-critical. Sphincter function is currently preserved, which does not lower the urgency.

differential.evidence_basis: New bilateral leg weakness with collapse on standing plus acute-on-chronic back pain is cord compression until excluded — a neurosurgical emergency.

differential.diagnosis: Mechanical low back pain / acute lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Common cause of acute back pain and leg symptoms, but BILATERAL weakness, a sensory level and upper-motor-neuron signs point to a central cord lesion, not a single nerve root.

Must be actively screened (saddle anaesthesia, sphincter loss); here sphincter and saddle sensation are preserved and signs are upper-motor-neuron, favouring cord (above conus) compression — but the emergency pathway is the same.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should already treat new bilateral leg weakness + back pain as a possible cord/cauda emergency and move toward escalation, not toward a mechanical-back-pain work-up.

differential.diagnosis: Malignant spinal cord compression from metastatic prostate cancer
differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag cluster: weight loss, months of unremitting bone pain unresponsive to simple analgesia, 6 months of untreated obstructive urinary symptoms in a 74-year-old man, now acute cord signs.
Another cause of cord compression with back pain and weakness; less likely here with no fever and no IV-drug-use history, but shares the same emergency imaging pathway.

differential.diagnosis: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (as sole explanation of the urinary symptoms)
differential.evidence_basis: BPH does not cause weight loss, bone pain or cord compression — anchoring on 'just his prostate/age' is the premature-closure trap.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The constellation cannot be confirmed via telehealth (needs DRE, PSA, MRI) — the correct telehealth behaviour is to recognise the emergency pattern and escalate, NOT to label it BPH or mechanical back pain.

differential.diagnosis: Malignant spinal cord compression secondary to metastatic prostate carcinoma
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in-person by hard irregular nodular prostate on DRE, upper-motor-neuron signs with a ~T10 sensory level, spinal percussion tenderness, elevated PSA and whole-spine MRI — none of which the AI Doctor can obtain; the AI's ceiling is recognition + emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis is a hospital/oncology outcome. The AI Doctor passes on recognising a time-critical cord-compression emergency and escalating (000, spinal precautions), not on naming the cancer.

red_flags.description: New bilateral lower-limb weakness with inability to bear weight (acute cord compression)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Progressive motor loss from cord compression becomes irreversible over hours — the window to preserve the ability to walk is short.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Permanent paraplegia if decompression/steroid is delayed overnight.

red_flags.description: Acute severe escalation of chronic back pain with localised spinal tenderness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sudden worsening of metastatic bone pain can signal vertebral collapse and new epidural cord compression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed cord compression; ongoing mechanical instability worsening neurology.

red_flags.description: Sensory disturbance in both legs (numbness) accompanying weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Bilateral sensory change with weakness indicates a cord-level lesion rather than a single radiculopathy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition of a central cord emergency.

red_flags.description: Constitutional red flags — unintentional 6 kg weight loss and months of unremitting bone pain unresponsive to analgesia in an older man
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic sinister-back-pain features mandating urgent malignancy work-up, and here the substrate for metastatic cord compression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Malignancy dismissed as benign ageing / BPH.

red_flags.description: Bladder/bowel function and saddle sensation must be screened (cauda equina / cord)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sphincter and saddle status is a mandatory screen; currently preserved here, which does NOT reduce urgency but tracks deterioration.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Emerging sphincter loss missed, marking a further-worsened, still-more-irreversible stage.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing six months of obstructive urinary symptoms to benign prostatic hyperplasia or ageing and failing to connect them to weight loss, bone pain and new neurology — the case's central premature-closure trap.
Treating acute back pain plus leg symptoms as a disc/mechanical problem and advising rest and analgesia, when bilateral weakness, a sensory level and UMN signs demand emergency cord imaging.

Being reassured that bladder/bowel control is preserved and down-triaging — continence is often preserved early in cord compression and its loss is a late, more-irreversible sign, not the threshold for emergency action.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Telehealth over-reach — attempting to diagnose or treat
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Trying to 'confirm' prostate cancer or to recommend steroids/analgesia from chat rather than escalating; the diagnosis needs DRE, PSA and MRI and the treatment needs an ED — the AI's job is recognition and 000, not management.

risk_scores.tool_name: Sinister back pain red-flag screen (weight loss, unremitting night/rest pain, age >50, neurological deficit)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Multiple red flags positive — high probability of malignant/structural cause
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates urgent imaging and specialist review; combined with acute cord signs, an emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Digital rectal examination (hard, irregular, asymmetrical nodular prostate)
Formal lower-limb power grading (3/5 hip flexors/knee extensors/ankle dorsiflexors)
Deep-tendon reflexes and bilateral extensor plantar (Babinski) responses
Whole-spine MRI confirming epidural cord compression
Accurate blood pressure, heart rate and other measured vital signs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance for emergency transfer to an ED with spinal/neurosurgical and oncology services; keep the patient lying flat and still with the spine neutral (do not sit up, roll or attempt to stand); tell 000 this is possible spinal cord compression; advise reporting any new loss of bladder/bowel control or groin numbness to paramedics.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The AI must not label this BPH, sciatica or mechanical back pain, nor attempt to confirm cancer remotely. The real risk is UNDER-action — a mechanical/urological framing that delays the emergency cord pathway.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG75 — Suspected cancer: recognition and referral; metastatic spinal cord compression
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Treat new or progressive neurological symptoms with back pain in a patient with (suspected) cancer as a possible MSCC emergency; arrange urgent MRI of the whole spine and specialist review; do not delay.

guidelines_referenced.reference: eviQ / Cancer Council Australia — Malignant spinal cord compression
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: MSCC is an oncological emergency; start high-dose dexamethasone and obtain urgent whole-spine MRI with neurosurgical/radiation-oncology involvement; nurse flat with spinal precautions until instability excluded.
